trajectory.description: Observation: Starting with empty workspace. Need to establish baseline pipeline. Analysis: The task is multivariate time series forecasting (7 features, 96->720). A simple LSTM encoder with MLP decoder can serve as baseline. I'll create model.py, train.py, predict.py, train for 5 epochs, and submit eval to validate pipeline and measure iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: CUDA kernel error during LSTM flatten_parameters due to cuDNN not compiled for GPU architecture. Analysis: The PyTorch installation's cuDNN version may be incompatible. Disabling cuDNN globally should resolve the issue and allow LSTM to run on GPU via native CUDA kernels. Decision: Add torch.backends.cudnn.enabled=False in both train.py and predict.py; retrain for 5 epochs and submit eval.

Observation: ReduceLROnPlateau verbose keyword error and previous CUDA kernel error. Analysis: PyTorch version likely pre-1.13; GPU kernels not compiled for this architecture. Decision: remove verbose, force CPU, set num_workers=0 to get pipeline working. Train 5 epochs and submit eval for baseline and time measurement.

trajectory.description: Observation: CPU training completed, best val MSE 1.388 (scaled). Epoch time ~7.4 min, total 37 min for 5 epochs. CUDA kernel error prevented GPU use earlier. Analysis: Need baseline test MSE first. GPU essential but LSTM cuDNN incompatible; simpler models may work on GPU. Time is very limited (0.248h remaining). Decision: Submit eval with current checkpoint to establish baseline score; then attempt GPU training with fast architecture (linear/transformer) in remaining time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline LSTM MSE=1.0521 on CPU (5 epochs). GPU was previously unusable due to cuDNN kernel error, but we can disable cuDNN and still use GPU for LSTM's fallback CUDA implementation. Analysis: With only ~14 min remaining, we can attempt GPU training for 2 epochs (likely ~2 min) to validate GPU speed and possibly improve score. Then submit eval. Decision: Modify train.py to set torch.backends.cudnn.enabled=False, device='cuda', and train for 2 epochs. Then evaluate.

trajectory.description: Observation: GPU is completely unusable due to CUDA kernel incompatibility; even basic CUDA ops fail. CPU is the only option. Remaining time budget ~14 min. Previous LSTM on CPU achieved MSE=1.0521 but took 37 min/5 epochs — too slow to improve now. Analysis: A faster, lightweight model (NLinear) can train on CPU in ~1-2 min and may outperform LSTM. NLinear uses only linear layers (720*96 per feature, shared) with <500k params and trains rapidly. Decision: replace model with NLinear, train 50 epochs, submit eval to get new baseline, and possibly iterate if time allows.

trajectory.command_output_summary: Training completed. Best val loss: 1.203178 at epoch 25
trajectory.description: Observation: NLinear (simple linear model) achieved MSE=0.472 in 57s for 42 epochs. Time remaining ~12 min. GPU unusable. Analysis: We can try more expressive but still fast linear model like DLinear (decomposition into trend + seasonal) which may outperform NLinear. We can train ~2-3 min and still iterate. Decision: Implement DLinear, add to model.py, update train/predict scripts, train 100 epochs with early stopping, submit eval.

Analysis: Need to evaluate DLinear to see if it improves over NLinear (MSE=0.4719). Time remaining ~9 min, enough for one more training iteration if score not satisfactory. Decision: Submit eval for DLinear checkpoint immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: DLinear test MSE 0.5051 (worse than NLinear 0.4719). Time remaining ~8 min. Analysis: NLinear original paper subtracts last input value to improve stationarity. Implementing that could boost performance. CPU training for NLinear is fast (~1.4 sec/epoch). Decision: add last-value normalization to NLinear, train 150 epochs, submit eval.
